coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Hongcaitai for selective detection of hypochlorite and mercuric ions and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lu-Shuang
Jiao, Xin-Yue
Zhang, Yu
Cheng, Cheng
Huang, Kun
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Natural source is an effective carbon precursor to produce carbon dots (CDs) with prominent physicochemical and photoluminescent properties. To enrich the CDs’ type and widen their applications, more natural sources are desired. Herein, Hongcaitai (Brassica compestris L. var. purpurea Bailey), one of the popular and cheap vegetables in Hubei province of China, was attempted as the carbon precursor for synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were divided into two parts based on different solubilities in ethanol, CDs-A (ethanol soluble) and CDs-B (ethanol insoluble). Both CDs possessed multi functionalities; however, CDs-B contained sulfur element while CDs-A did not. Fluorescence quenching occured for both CDs in the presence of ClO− and Hg2+. However, CDs-A were more sensitive towards ClO−, while CDs-B were more sensitive towards Hg2+. Thus, CDs-A were successful to determine ClO− in tap water with limit of detection at 0.015 μM. CDs-B were successful to determine Hg2+ in river water with limit of detection at 0.06 μM. The results demonstrate that the sub-classfication of CDs by the subtle different properties, e.g. ethanol solubility, is necessary for better application performance of CDs. In addition, thanks to their low toxicity, both CDs were suitable for cellular labelling on HepG2 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hongcaitai
Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
Mercuric ion
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A
Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ by CDs-B
Applications of CDs as fluorescence sensor for the detection of ClO− or Hg2+
Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A
Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ by CDs-B

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of CDs-A and CDs-B by hydrothermal method from Hongcaitai. (a) and (b) are the photographs of CDs-A and CDs-B under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right), respectively.
QYs of crude CDs prepared at different (a) hydrothermal temperature, (b) time duration and (c) weight of Hongcaitai powder in 10 mL H2O.

TEM images and size distributions of (a) CDs-A and (b) CDs-B. Insets in (a) and (b): high-resolution TEM images of CDs-A and CDs-B. (c) XRD patterns of CDs-A and CDs-B. (d) FTIR spectra of CDs-A and CDs-B.
Full scan XPS spectra of (a) CDs-A and (b) CDs-B. High resolution spectra of CDs-A (a-I, II, III) and CDs-B (b-I, II, III, IV).

UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of (a) CDs-A and (b) CDs-B, respectively. Fluorescence spectra of (c) CDs-A and (d) CDs-B with excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm by an increment of 10 nm. Insets in (c) and (d): normalized fluorescence spectra of CDs-A and CDs-B. (e) The fluorescence decay curve of CDs-A and CDs-B.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs-A and (b) CDs-B solutions with different pH values. Insets in (a) and (b): effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-A and CDs-B. Fluorescence spectra of (c) CDs-A and (d) CDs-B dispersed in various concentrations of NaCl. Insets in (c) and (d): effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-A and CDs-B. (e) Photostability of CDs-A and CDs-B.
Influence of (a) pH, (b) type of buffers, (c) temperature, and (d) reaction time on the quenching efficiency (F/F0) of CDs-A and CDs-B at 50 μM of detection target.

(a) Selectivity of CDs-A for ClO− over other ions (Xn±) and the interference of them. (b) Selectivity of CDs-B for Hg2+ over other ions (Xn±) and the interference of them. (c) The relationship between F/F0 and concentration of ClO− from 0 to100 μM. Inset in (c): fluorescence spectra of the CDs-A solution upon addition of various concentrations of ClO−. (d) The linear plot with the concentration of ClO− from 0.05 to 15 μM. (e) The relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 200 μM. Inset in (e): fluorescence spectra of the CDs-B solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+. (f) The linear plot with the concentration of Hg2+ from 0.2 to 15 μM.
Cell viability of HepG2 cells in the presence of different concentrations of CDs-A and CDs-B with DMSO as the positive control.
Confocal microscopy images of HepG2 cells labeled with CDs-A (a, b, c) and CDs-B (d, e, f) with different excitation wavelengths at 405, 488 and 559 nm, respectively.
Comparison of different CDs for ClO− detection.
Detection of ClO− in tap water.
Comparison of different CDs for Hg2+ detection.
Detection of Hg2+ in river water.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Hongcaitai for selective detection of hypochlorite and mercuric ions and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural source is an effective carbon precursor to produce carbon dots (CDs) with prominent physicochemical and photoluminescent properties. To enrich the CDs’ type and widen their applications, more natural sources are desired. Herein, Hongcaitai (Brassica compestris L. var. purpurea Bailey), one of the popular and cheap vegetables in Hubei province of China, was attempted as the carbon precursor for synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were divided into two parts based on different solubilities in ethanol, CDs-A (ethanol soluble) and CDs-B (ethanol insoluble). Both CDs possessed multi functionalities; however, CDs-B contained sulfur element while CDs-A did not. Fluorescence quenching occured for both CDs in the presence of ClO− and Hg2+. However, CDs-A were more sensitive towards ClO−, while CDs-B were more sensitive towards Hg2+. Thus, CDs-A were successful to determine ClO− in tap water with limit of detection at 0.015 μM. CDs-B were successful to determine Hg2+ in river water with limit of detection at 0.06 μM. The results demonstrate that the sub-classfication of CDs by the subtle different properties, e.g. ethanol solubility, is necessary for better application performance of CDs. In addition, thanks to their low toxicity, both CDs were suitable for cellular labelling on HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of emerging fluorescent carbon materials with the size under 10 nm, have drawn researchers’ numerous attentions since their initial discovery in 2004 [1]. CDs possess many fascinating properties, such as tunable excitation and emission fluorescence, chemical inertness and high photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, good aqueous solubility and easy modification [2,3]. Owing to these advantages, CDs were successfully utilized in a variety of fields, such as fluorescent ink, imaging, fluorescent sensor, catalyst, optoelectronic device and so on [4–12].

The photoluminescent and physicochemical properties of CDs were largely dependent on the carbon precursors [13–16]. Doping and functionalizing with some specific heteroatoms or compounds could adjust the CDs’ properties [17–20]. Natural organics vary in chemical compositions with abundant heteroatoms, which are ideal carbon precursors for CDs [21–23]. Therefore, to enrich fluorescent CDs’ type and widen their applications, the exploration of new natural sources for CDs is still a hot topic. Furthermore, green chemistry is becoming more and more attractive because of its environmentally friendly and sustainable feature. From this point, preparation of CDs with prominent physicochemical and photoluminescent properties using green carbon precursor and green synthetic method is more desirable.

Hongcaitai (Brassica compestris L. var. purpurea Bailey) is one of the popular and cheap vegetables in Hubei province of China, which is rich in protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fiber and vitamins, etc [24]. In the present study, Hongcaitai was chosen as a natural carbon source to yield CDs by hydrothermal method without any further passivation or functionalization. The obtained CDs were divided into two parts, one of which was both water and ethanol soluble, named as CDs-A; the other part was only water soluble, named as CDs-B. The CDs had high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent stability to pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation. Particularly, CDs-A and CDs-B showed different selectivities towards hypochlorite (ClO−) and mercuric ion (Hg2+), and were applied to detect them, respectively. Furthermore, owing to their low cytotoxicity, both of them succeeded in cell imaging.

Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KNO3, NaNO3, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, AgNO3, NiSO4·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnSO4·H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, NaCl, KBr, KI, Na2SO3, NaNO2, Na2SO4, Na3PO4·12H2O, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaAcO, NaIO3, NaClO3, NaClO2 and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) aqueous solution were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). Hongcaitai was bought from the local market (Wuhan, Hubei, China). The human hepatoma cell line (HepG2 cells) was obtained from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using Hongcaitai as carbon precursor. In a typical process, the Hongcaitai was first thoroughly washed with ultrapure water and cut into small pieces. After drying at 80 °C, the pieces were mechanically ground into fine powder. Then, 0.5 g powder and 10 mL ultrapure water were added into a hydrothermal reaction kettle and heated at 240 °C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained product was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles, and the filtrate was extracted with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic components. Afterwards, the aqueous phase containing CDs was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) against ultrapure water for 24 h with renewing the water every 6 h to remove the small molecules. The final solution was vacuum evaporated and dried at 80 °C to yield the solid. Anhydrous ethanol was then added to the solid CDs. Part of them was dissolved in ethanol, which was named as CDs-A; while the rest ethanol-undissolved part was called CDs-B. The two parts were separately collected and dried at 80 °C for further characterization and usage.

Quantum yield (QY) was measured with quinine sulfate as the reference (QY of 0.54 at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4). The CDs and quinine sulfate were diluted to six different concentrations with ultrapure water (refractive index (η) of 1.33) and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (η=1.33), respectively. The solutions were subjected to the absorbance and fluorescence measurements excited at 360 nm. To minimize the self-absorption effect, the absorbance value of each solution was maintained below 0.1. The QYs of CDs were calculated using the Eq. (1),
(1) QYS = (QYR·KS·ηS 2)/(KR·ηR 2)

where QY is quantum yield; η is the refractive index of the solvent; K is the slope of the linear fitted curve of fluorescence integrated area versus absorbance. The subscript R and S stand for the reference (quinine sulfate) and sample (CDs), respectively.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were collected on an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured by a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime was measured by a Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, USA). The pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation stability of CDs-A and CDs-B were evaluated individually. For pH stability test, the pH of the CDs solution was regulated with HCl or NaOH solution to the desired value. The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated in the presence of NaCl from 0 to 1.0 M. The photostability of CDs was investigated through continuous irradiation for 2 h at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The fluorescence spectra and/or intensity of these solutions were recorded. The fluorescence intensity was obtained at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-A were used for the detection of ClO−. For the typical process, 300 μL sodium phosphate buffer (PBS, 0.2 M, pH 3.0), 200 μL CDs-A solution (80 μg mL−1) and 2.5 mL of working or sample solution were vortex-mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was then measured at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The working solutions of ClO− were freshly prepared by diluting the stock solution with ultrapure water to the desired concentrations.

For the selectivity experiment, some cations and anions which may potentially cause interference to the detection were chosen to assess their impact on fluorescence intensity of CDs individually at the respective concentration of 50 μM. Furthermore, to assess the co-existing interference, each ion was introduced into the ClO− (50 μM) detecting system separately. The fluorescence intensity was collected at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the CDs-A for detection of ClO− were studied by parallel measurements of standard working solution of ClO− (1 μM, 5 μM and 10 μM) using the same batch and three different batches of CDs-A respectively, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated.

The developed method was used to determine ClO− in tap water, which was collected in our laboratory and analyzed directly without any other treatment. For the recovery test, the tap water was spiked with the known concentration of ClO−, and the recovery was calculated using Eq. (2),
(2) recovery (%) = Cmeasured/(Cadded + C0) × 100%
where Cmeasured is the concentration calculated by the fitted regression equation, Cadded is the spiked concentration and C0 stands for the actual concentration of ClO− in tap water. All of the measurements were performed in triplicate.
For comparison, the tap water was also analyzed by the most commonly used NN-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) colorimetric method by determining the UV absorbance of the samples at 515 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ by CDs-B
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-B were applied for sensing Hg2+. Typically, 300 μL PBS (0.2 M, pH 3.0), 200 μL CDs-B solution (120 μg mL−1), 1.0 mL of working or sample solution and 1.5 mL ultrapure water were vortex-mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence measurement and selectivity, repeatability and reproducibility experiments were similar to fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A, as described in Section 2.6.

The developed method was used to determine Hg2+ in river water, which was collected from the Yangtse River (Wuhan, China). Before analysis, the silver nitrate (the final concentration was 500 μM in the sample solution) was first added to the river water to remove the possible interfering ions (e.g. SO3 2−, Cl−, Br− and I−), and then the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The recovery test was carried out the same as fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A. For comparison, the river water was also analyzed by the DMA-80 Direct Mercury Analyzer (Milestone Srl., Italy).

CDs-A and CDs-B underwent the same cellular imaging and cytotoxicity test with HepG2 cells as the typical representative. For the cell imaging test, first, the cells were seeded in the 12-well plate with glass cover slips and cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fatal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C and 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h for adherence. Then the culture medium was discarded, and the cells were incubated in the fresh culture medium with CDs at 20 μg mL−1 for another 1 h. Finally, the medium was removed and the cells were fixed with 4% formaldehyde and washed with PBS to remove the free and physically adsorbed CDs. The fluorescence images of HepG2 cells were obtained on a confocal fluorescence microscopy (Olympus FV1200, Japan) at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 559 nm, respectively.

In vitro cytotoxicity of CDs for HepG2 cells was assessed by the MTT assay. In brief, 100 μL HepG2 cells in the culture medium at the density of 5 × 104 mL−1 were seeded in the 96-well plate per well for 24 h incubation. Then the culture medium was discarded and the cells were incubated in the fresh culture medium containing CDs with different concentrations (0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100 and 500 μg mL−1) for another 24 h, and 1% (v/v) DMSO was selected as the positive control. After that, 10 μL MTT (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added into each well for another 4 h incubation, followed by the addition of 100 μL DMSO to each well. Finally, the absorbance was measured by a microplate reader (Biotek, USA) at a wavelength of 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated according to the Eq. (3),
(3) cell viability (%) = (Aexperimental/Acontrol) × 100%
where Aexperimental and Acontrol are the absorbance of cells incubated in the presence and absence of CDs, respectively.

Hydrothermal is one of the most widely used green synthetic methods for CDs due to its convenience and easy operation without extra chemical reagents or apparatus [25]. Herein, it was adopted for the preparation of CDs with Hongcaitai as carbon precursor, and the general process is illustrated in Scheme 1 . As reported previously, during the hydrothermal process, the carbonaceous natural compounds may go through dehydration, polymerization and carbonization [22]. Hydrothermal temperature and time duration may have significant effects on the properties of resulting CDs [25]. In order to achieve CDs with high quantum yield, the hydrothermal temperature, time duration and the ratio of the carbon precursor to water were optimized, successively. The QY of the crude product, which was obtained only after filtration following the hydrothermal treatment, was evaluated as an indicator.

As shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), the QY of CDs reached the maximum at the hydrothermal temperature and time duration of 240 °C and 20 h, respectively. According to the previous reports [25], low reaction temperature and short reaction time duration may cause low reaction efficiency and insufficient carbonization of the precursor, thus leading to the low QY. On the contrary, at much higher hydrothermal temperature and longer time duration, QY may be still reduced as a result of the reducing emissive sites, i.e. surface functional groups [26]. Furthermore, the mass of the Hongcaitai powder in water was evaluated (Fig. 1(c)). Obviously, the QY of the obtained CDs was relatively low in the case of a lower precursor mass (0.1 g), and higher when the Hongcaitai mass ranged from 0.2 to 1.0 g with a little variance. This observation may be because of the slow reaction rate of chemical components in Hongcaitai at the initial low concentrations [25]. Considering too much Hongcaitai powder in the synthetic system may result in the inhomogeneity problem, the Hongcaitai mass was fixed at 0.5 g. In summary, the optimum synthetic conditions were 0.5 g Hongcaitai powder in 10 mL water with reaction temperature of 240 °C for 20 h. After filtration, the obtained crude solution had a QY of 21.0%, which was relatively higher than most other reported CDs synthesized by natural source [21–23].

As for the purification of CDs, filtration, dichloromethane extraction and dialysis, were applied in sequence [27,28], and the solid CDs were obtained after drying. It was interesting to find that, in our experiment, although the CDs were well aqueous soluble, they were only partially dissolved in ethanol. Hence, the CDs were further divided into two parts as described in Section 2.2. The ethanol soluble CDs-A exhibited the higher prominent fluorescence QY (17.8%) than the ethanol insoluble CDs-B (12.1%), probably attributing to the more emission sites on CDs-A than CDs-B. Both QYs of CDs-A and CDs-B were lower than the crude product, perhaps due to the loss of CDs or some organics with fluorescence during the purification processes. In addition, as discussed below (Section 3.4), the detection selectivity and/or sensitivity of the two CDs differed a lot. These phenomena may be owing to the different types and/or amounts of surface functional groups on the two distinct CDs, which manifests the necessity of classification of the obtained CDs by ethanol solubility.

The morphologies of CDs-A and CDs-B were observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), both CDs-A and CDs-B had uniform quasi spherical shape with a narrow size distribution of 1.6-2.3 nm centered at 1.9 nm for CDs-A (Fig. 2(a) inset) and 1.9-3.3 nm centered at 2.6 nm for CDs-B (Fig. 2(b) inset). The close observation images (Fig. 2(a) and (b) −inset) clearly revealed that both CDs-A and CDs-B possessed the crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (102) plane of graphitic carbon [29]. The typical XRD patterns of both CDs-A and CDs-B (Fig. 2(c)) presented a broad peak centered at 22° associated with the graphitic structure [30], which was consistent with the TEM results.

The FTIR and XPS were employed to evaluate the chemical compositions and surface functionalities of the CDs. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2(d)), CDs-A and CDs-B shared some similar absorption peaks, e.g. 3375 cm−1 (ν OH/NH), 2933 cm−1 (ν CH), 1668 cm−1 (ν CO), 1575 cm−1 (ν CC) and 1420 cm−1 (ν CNHC) [18,19,23]. The difference was that the absorption peaks at 1303 cm−1 and 1129 cm−1 attributing to the −CO and CN/C-S were only observed in CDs-B [14,17,18].

The XPS full scan spectrum of CDs-A (Fig. 3 (a)) displayed three typical peaks at 284.81 eV, 532.06 eV and 399.91 eV, which are attributed to C 1 s (71.91%), O 1 s (25.24%) and N 1 s (2.85%), respectively [17]. As shown in Fig. 3(a-I), the high resolution C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four subpeaks at 284.56 eV, 285.00 eV, 285.91 eV and 288.45 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO bonds [14]. The spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 3(a-II)) displayed two major subpeaks at 531.70 eV and 532.33 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [19,20]. The high resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(a-III)) presented two major subpeaks at 399.31 eV and 400.19 eV, corresponding to NC and NH [19], respectively.

As displayed in Fig. 3(b), the full scan spectrum of CDs-B was characteristic of a peak of S 2p (168.84 eV), besides the dominant peaks of C 1 s (284.80 eV), O 1 s (531.33 eV) and N 1 s (399.74 eV), which manifests the presence of sulfur in CDs-B. Elemental analysis reveals that CDs-B contained 63.09% C, 32.42% O, 3.34% N and 1.15% S. As shown in Fig. 3(b-I), the C 1 s spectrum mainly consisted of five subpeaks at 284.47 eV, 285.19 eV, 286.14 eV, 287.70 eV and 288.52 eV, which may be assigned to CC/CC, C–S, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively [19,30]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(b-II)) was composed of two subpeaks at 530.97 eV and 532.04 eV, which are from CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [15]. N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(b-III)) displayed two subpeaks at 399.47 eV and 400.11 eV, corresponding to NC and NH, respectively [19]. The high resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3(b-IV)) exhibited two major subpeaks at 168.64 eV and 169.72 eV, corresponding to −C-SOX- (x = 2, 3, 4) groups [17–19,31].

Both FTIR and XPS results disclose that abundant hydrophilic functional groups, such as −OH, −COOH and −NH2, may be present on the surfaces of both CDs, which not only improved the water solubility but also provided the interaction sites for specific ions or compounds [19]. Compared to CDs-A, CDs-B particularly contained sulfur in addition to higher contents of oxygen and nitrogen, which may be the reason why CDs-A and CDs-B possessed different solubilities in ethanol and different sensitivities towards Hg2+ and ClO− (Section 3.4).
In summary, the prepared CDs were nearly quasi spheres, carbon-rich with doping of nitrogen and/or sulfur, and abundant with many functional groups.

The aqueous solutions of both CDs-A (Scheme 1(a)) and CDs-B (Scheme 1(b)) were faint yellow and completely transparent under daylight, and emitted strong blue light under UV irradiation at 365 nm. To further characterize the photoluminescent properties, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs-A and CDs-B in aqueous solution were measured.

As depicted in Fig. 4 (a), there was an obvious peak at 283 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs-A, owing to n–π* transition of CO [23,32]. However, a broad absorption band ranging from 200 nm to 500 nm was observed in the spectrum of CDs-B (Fig. 4(b)), which was ascribed to π–π* transition of CC and n–π* transition of CO and/or other chromophores [18,33].

The fluorescence spectra of CDs-A and CDs-B were almost the same, with a broad peak in both the excitation and emission spectra, as depicted in Fig. 4(a) and (b). The excitation dependent photoluminescence phenomena were both observed for CDs-A and CDs-B; the red shift of the emission peak corresponding to the gradually increadsing excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm was also observed according to the normalized emission spectra in Fig. 4(c) and (d) −inset. This behavior of CDs was probably related to the different sizes of CDs or emissive sites resulting from the different chemical groups on the surface of the CDs [5,10,34]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of them first increased and then decreased with the maximum located at the excitation of 330 nm (Fig. 4(c) and (d)).

Fig. 4(e) exhibited the fluorescence decay curve of CDs (330 nm excitation, 410 nm emission). The average lifetime of CDs-A was determined to be 10.06 ns with three components: 17.24 ns (48.02%), 4.33 ns (40.32%) and 0.33 ns (11.66%). The average lifetime of CDs-B was calculated to be 10.93 ns, a little longer than that of CDs-A, consisting of three components: 4.71 ns (53.87%), 27.51 ns (30.37%) and 0.27 ns (15.76%). The multi-exponential nature of the lifetime demonstrated that there are different emissive sites on CDs [10,35,36].

The fluorescence stability of CDs in terms of the pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation is reflected in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded in solution pH range from 1 to 13. CDs-A and CDs-B responded stable fluorescence in pH 5–9, and only a slight decrease of fluorescence intensity in a strong acidic or basic media was observed (Fig. 5(a) and (b)). In addition, a slight blue shift of emission upon pH changing from acidic to basic was also observed, possibly due to the fact that CDs served as both electron donors and acceptors, and environmental H+ or OH− may participate the electron transfer process [37]. As depicted in Fig. 5(c) and (d), ionic strength posed virtually no effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-B, while only a slight decrease was observed for CDs-A at a much higher concentration of NaCl (e.g. 1.0 M), reflecting the excellent stability of them in high-salt conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(e), after continuous photo irradiation for 2 h, no significant change of the fluorescence intensity of CDs occurred, suggesting their excellent photostability. Summing up the above, the CDs synthesized herein were featured with outstanding pH and ionic strength stabilities in addition to their high resistance to photobleaching, making them promising candidates for labelling and sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs as fluorescence sensor for the detection of ClO− or Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiment, the fluorescence of CDs before divided into two parts was found to be quenched only by ClO− and Hg2+, demonstrating a high selectivity of CDs. After the CDs were divided into two parts based on the solubility in ethanol, the selectivity was further enhanced. The quenching efficiencies by ClO− and Hg2+ were both evaluated with F/F0 as the response value (where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution in the presence and absence of target ion, respectively). In the case of CDs-A, compared to the crude CDs, F/F0 decreased from 0.40 to 0.27 for ClO−, but increased from 0.63 to 0.77 for Hg2+; while, on the contrary, in the case of CDs-B, F/F0 increased from 0.40 to 0.58 for ClO−, but decreased from 0.63 to 0.56 for Hg2+. These results demonstrate that CDs-A were more sensitive towards ClO−, while CDs-B were more sensitive towards Hg2+. The fluorescence of CDs-A quenched by ClO− may be attribute to the redox reaction destroying the surface passivation of the CDs-A. While the fluorescence of CDs-B quenched by Hg2+ may be attribute to the non-radiative electron-transfer. Also based on the above characterization of the two CDs (section 3.2), more reductive groups may be present on CDs-A, while the sulfur containing groups would enhance the affinity between CDs-B and Hg2+. Therefore, CDs-A were utilized for sensing ClO−, and CDs-B for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs-A
ce:section.ce:para: In order to establish the best detecting condition with higher sensitivity, the effect of pH value, the type of buffers, reaction temperature and time were investigated successively. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the fluorescence of CDs-A was more efficiently quenched at the acidic conditions by ClO− with minimum F/F0 at pH 3.0. The oxidative property of the ClO− may be responsible for the quenching [38]. At low pH, a stronger oxidant, HClO, mainly existed, which would lead to the prominent fluorescence quenching due to the possible oxidation of reductive groups on the CDs, e.g. hydroxyl. With the increasing pH, the content of ClO− instead of HClO would increase; the oxidative ability of ClO− was weaker than that of HClO, and the fluorescence quenching was thus weakened [38]. Moreover, different types of buffers were used to adjust the solution to pH 3.0. As shown in Fig. 6(b), except for Tris-HCl, all the other three buffers, i.e. Britton-Robison (BR), acetic acid-sodium acetate (HAcO-NaAcO) and PBS can achieve equal quenching of the fluorescence. PBS was finally selected in the following experiment for ClO− detection for its popularity and compatibility with most sample matrixes. Furthermore, the effects of reaction temperature (20 °C–60 °C) and reaction time (1 min–60 min) were investigated. The temperature exerted no apparent impact on the quenching efficiency (Fig. 6(c)), and the steady F/F0 was reached in about 10 min (Fig. 6(d)). Taking into account of the convenience and sensitivity of the detection, the optimal conditions were as follows: PBS was applied to adjust the sample pH to 3.0, and the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 10 min.

The selectivity of the CDs-A for ClO− was evaluated by studying the fluorescence quenching in the presence of individual suspect ions. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), apparent changes of the F/F0 values were observed only for Hg2+ and ClO−, with a much lower value in the case of ClO−, reflecting a higher selectivity and sensitivity to ClO−. In order to further estimate the interference of coexisting ions, the ClO− and each suspect ion at the concentration of 50 μM were simultaneously added into the detection solution. Among those, Fe2+, SO3 2− and NO2 − showed some suppression on the fluorescence quenching. Probably thanks to the reducibility of these ions, they may react with the oxidant ClO−, and thus lower the available concentration of ClO− to CDs-A. However, considering the fact that these reductive ions cannot coexist with ClO− in a genuine sample, the interference of them to ClO− could be neglected.

According to the above experimental results, it was reasonable to set CDs-A as the sensor for ClO−. As reflected in Fig. 7(c), the fluorescence of CDs-A was gradually quenched corresponding to the increasing ClO− concentration, but with the consistent maximum fluorescence emission wavelength. A good linear relationship was obtained between the F/F0 and ClO− concentration within the range of 0.05 μM–15 μM (Fig. 7(d)). The limit of detection (LOD) for the ClO− was calculated to be 0.015 μM. In comparison with the reported methods using CDs for detection of ClO− as listed in Table 1 , comparable sensitivity and linear ranges were obtained.

The RSD values for three parallel measurements of standard solutions of ClO− at the concentration of 1 μM, 5 μM and 10 μM for the same batch of CDs-A were 4.9%, 3.1% and 0.7%, respectively; while, the RSD values were 3.3%, 2.7% and 1.7% in the case of three different batches of CDs-A. The method showed good repeatability and reproducibility for detection of ClO−.

The established method was applied to the direct determination of ClO− in tap water. The ClO− concentration was determined to be 2.66 μM, which was nearly the same with the result analyzed by the DPD colorimetric method, 2.57 μM. According to statistical analysis by Student t-test, P value was 0.12, larger than 0.05, demonstrating that there was no significant difference between the results of the two methods. Furthermore, as listed in Table 2 , it could be clearly seen that the recoveries of ClO− ranged from 97% to 107% with the RSDs of 2.2–5.2%, further proving the accuracy and reliability of this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ by CDs-B
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the Section 3.4.1, the fluorescence detection of Hg2+ by CDs-B was optimized. The optimum detection conditions were as follows: PBS was applied to adjust the pH value to 3.0, the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 10 min. As for the selectivity (Fig. 7(b)), no apparent quenching of the fluorescence of CDs-B was observed except by Hg2+ and ClO−, which forcefully suggested that the CDs-B had high selectivity. The quenching of fluorescence by Hg2+ was attributed to its interaction with the functional groups on the surface of CDs-B resulting in the non-radiative electron-transfer. As the CDs-B may possess more sulfur containing functional groups according to the FTIR and XPS results as discussed in Section 3.2, which may facilitate the electron transfer, CDs-B were more sensitive and suitable to detect Hg2+ [18].

As clearly shown in Fig. 7(b), for detection of Hg2+, the other coexisting ions except SO3 2−, Cl−, Br− and I−, displayed no obvious interference. The inhibition of these four ions on the quenching may be because of the stronger affinity of them to Hg2+ than the functional groups on the surface of CDs. Considering this, to detect Hg2+ in genuine water, the pretreatment with silver nitrate to remove the interfering ions was necessary.

The fluorescence quenching of CDs-B by Hg2+ was obviously enhanced upon increasing the Hg2+ concentration from 0.2 μM to 200 μM (Fig. 7(e)). The F/F0 exhibited a good linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.2–15 μM (Fig. 7(f)). The LOD of Hg2+ was calculated to be 0.06 μM. As listed in Table 3 , compared with the reported studies using CDs as a sensor for Hg2+, the proposed method had moderate sensitivity which may be useful in environmental applications for Hg2+ detection.

The RSD values for three parallel measurements of standard solutions of Hg2+ at the concentration of 1 μM, 5 μM and 10 μM for the same batch of CDs-B were 1.7%, 2.5% and 1.9%, respectively; while, the RSD values were 3.0%, 3.7% and 4.3% in the case of three different batches of CDs-B. The method showed good repeatability and reproducibility for detection of Hg2+.

The proposed method was used for the detection of Hg2+ in river water, and no Hg2+ or below the LOD of the method was found. The river water was also analyzed with the Direct Mercury Analyzer, showing that the concentration of Hg2+ was lower than the LOD (1 nM) of that method. As listed in Table 4 , it could be clearly seen that the recoveries of Hg2+ were in the range of 98–109% with the RSDs of 1.7–3.4%, further demonstrating the good accuracy and reliability of this assay.

As shown in Fig. 8 , MTT study revealed that the average cell viability was greater than 94% at a concentration of CDs-A up to 500 μg mL−1. The cell viability was higher than 97% when CDs-B concentration was below 100 μg mL−1 and decreased slightly to 82% at a concentration up to 500 μg mL−1. The results suggested that both CDs-A and CDs-B exerted low toxicity to HepG2 cells at a relatively high concentration which can potentially be used for cell imaging or other biomedical applications.

For cell imaging test, CDs at the concentration of 20 μg mL−1 was used. The fluorescence images (Fig. 9 ) taken after 1 h incubation reflected that the cytoplasm in cells emitted bright blue, green and red fluorescence when excited with 405, 488 and 559 nm laser, respectively. The phenomena demonstrate that both CDs-A and CDs-B easily penetrated the cell membrane and labeled the cytoplasm, meanwhile with a small amount into the nuclei [13]. The multicolor emission provided a wide range of wavelength for observation, also making CDs an ideal cellular imaging agent [18].

In this study, for the first time, Hongcaitai was used as carbon precursor for fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal method. The CDs were separated into two parts according to the solubility in ethanol, i.e. CDs-A (ethanol soluble) and CDs-B (ethanol insoluble). The characterization of them demonstrated that the composition of their surface functional groups were different. However, both CDs possessed excellent fluorescent stability to pH, high ionic strength and photo irradiation. In addition, CDs-A were more sensible for ClO− while CDs-B were more sensible for Hg2+. Thus, the CDs-A and CDs-B were used to determine ClO− in tap water and Hg2+ in river water, respectively. Additionally, due to the low cytotoxicity of CDs, they succeeded in cell imaging of HepG2 cells, demonstrating that they were both potential cellular imaging agents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31471208 & 31671195) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017KFYXJJ149).

sb:author.ce:surname: National Institute of Nutrition and Food Safety, China CDC

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Lu-shuang Li is a PhD student in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Her research topic focuses on the synthesis of novel carbon dots for multiple applications.

Prof. Li Xu is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD degree from National University of Singapore in 2009. Her research interests focus on separation and analysis of complex samples and application of functional materials in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.
